text:
Aerosol samples (TSP) were collected at the summit of Mount Tai (1534 m a.s.l.) in the North China Plain from May to June 2006 to investigate the impact of agricultural waste burning on free tropospheric chemistry. The samples were analyzed for water-soluble dicarboxylic acids, ketocarboxylic acids, and $\alpha$-dicarbonyls using gas chromatography and GC-MS. Total dicarboxylic acids (C2–C11) concentrations ranged from 220 to 6070 ng m$^{−3}$, with oxalic acid (C2) as the dominant species, followed by succinic or malonic acids. Other detected compounds included unsaturated aliphatic diacids, aromatic diacids, $\omega$-oxocarboxylic acids (primarily glyoxylic acid), pyruvic acid, and $\alpha$-dicarbonyls (glyoxal and methylglyoxal). 

The concentrations observed at the mountaintop were significantly higher—often by several factors—than those reported at ground level in major Chinese megacities. Temporal variations showed a sharp increase in organic acid levels matching the peak of the wheat straw burning season in early June, followed by a decline when air masses shifted from southerly to northerly origins. These fluctuations were consistent with trends in total carbon (TC) and water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC). The high abundances of these compounds are attributed to both direct emissions from biomass burning and the subsequent photochemical oxidation of volatile and semi-volatile precursors. Ratios of specific compounds suggests that while the aerosols were influenced by fresh burning emissions, significant secondary processing occurred during transport. This study highlights that seasonal agricultural fires profoundly influence the organic aerosol loading and air quality of the free troposphere over East China.
